title: Overflow Tables
text: Overflow Tables DBMSs may impose upper limits to the number of fields in a table, the length of a field, and the length of a record. Some DBMSs do not support variable-length fields with a high maximum length. To overcome these limitations, Uniface provides an overflow mechanism, in which data that is represented as a single modeled entity is automatically split into two tables—the base table and an overflow table. The base table contains all the fixed-length fields plus the first part of the variable data in the occurrence. The overflow table contains the rest of the variable-length data. An overflow table contains the primary key from the base table and the overflow information from the field that causes the overflow table. The name of an overflow table consists of the same name as the lowercase entity name with the prefix o . Note: The overflow mechanism is a technical encoding for larger amounts of data and is not transparent for other applications. When transparency is required, use segmented fields that result in Text and Image data types. For more information, see Segmented Fields: String Packing Codes (SC and SU) . The amount of variable-length data that is maintained in the base table depends on the DBMS. Uniface automatically creates overflow tables in the following circumstances: When the entity contains one or more fields defined using variable-length packing codes: C* R* U* W* VC* VR* VU* VW* When the entity contains one or more fields defined using segmented string fields (packing codes ‘SC*’ and so on), and the DBMS for that entity does not support this feature. When the data size of the occurrence being written is larger than the maximum size supported by the DBMS in use. The Uniface connector interface automatically performs all the I/O needed to create and maintain overflow tables and to store and retrieve occurrences from it. CAUTION: If overflow tables are created, remember to back them up with the rest of your data. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
